title: Unseen Australia
creator: Chris Tangey
award_or_showcase_provenance: Independent aerial/drone cinematographer Chris Tangey (Alice Springs Film & Television, Northern Territory, Australia) -- 'Unseen Australia' (2020) won a Gold Plaque from the Australian Cinematographers Society (ACS) and 'Best Drone Film' at the 2020 Paris Art & Movie Awards (PAMA), and was a Landscape-category nominee referenced by a 2021 international drone film festival YouTube reupload. Tangey is separately, independently documented (FilmFreeway, IMDb, Stage32, ProAV Express, dronesforhire.com.au) as one of the most awards-decorated independent drone cinematographers of 2020-2024 worldwide, including a Landscape-category win at the Jeju International Drone Film Festival (South Korea, 2021) and a Best Cinematography win at the Tokyo Film Awards (2022), with his outback-Australia aerial work also screened at World Expo Osaka 2025.
notes: MANUALLY CURATED WORKED EXAMPLE -- one exemplar only, per spec AC3.4/Scope-out (no automated scraping/bulk downloading was performed to produce this record). Verified this session via YouTube's oEmbed endpoint (https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=...&format=json), which confirmed the exact title ('Unseen Australia') and channel/author (Chris Tangey, https://www.youtube.com/@christangey) for this URL -- i.e. the video sits on the cinematographer's own channel, not a third-party reupload. Web search corroboration (FilmFreeway's 'Unseen Australia' and 'Chris Tangey' profile pages, an IMDb short-film title page for 'Unseen Australia (Short 2020)', a Stage32 bio, a ProAV Express customer profile, and dronesforhire.com.au) independently confirms Tangey as a real, independent, Alice-Springs-based (outback Australia) aerial/drone cinematographer, and this specific short as ACS Gold Plaque winner (2020) and 'Best Drone Film' at the 2020 Paris Art & Movie Awards. A second video, oEmbed-verified separately this session (video id BHQ2S9H-xKo, titled '2021 LANDSCAPE NOMINEE - Unseen Australia by Chris Tangey', uploaded by the YouTube channel 'Boston Drone Film Festival'), corroborates a Landscape-category festival nomination for this same film. Together this satisfies the assignment's 'notable recognition (festival selection ... )' bar via verifiable festival selections/awards rather than an arbitrary personal upload. `license_category` is set to the conservative default 'all-rights-reserved' because no explicit Creative Commons marking on this specific upload was verified in this session -- consequently `local_media_path` stays null and no video file was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo. `duration` is left null: YouTube's oEmbed response does not include a duration field, and no other verified-duration source was consulted this session -- rather than fabricate a plausible-looking number, this field is left unset pending a real YouTube Data API call or manual playback confirmation. `scores` (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are `scores_provenance: manually_estimated` placeholder judgments based on the video's documented genre and pedigree (professional, multi-award-winning cinematic aerial footage of the Australian outback), NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the same no-bulk-download constraint. `composite_score` IS a real computation: it was produced by calling this repo's own `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score` with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.82, exposure=0.80, motion_smoothness=0.83) and `drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights()` (weights_version 'default-v1', equal 1/3 weighting on sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness, composition weight fixed at 0.0), run via the project venv (.venv/bin/python) -- so the composite arithmetic itself is genuine and reproducible (result: 0.8167), even though its inputs are estimates rather than measurements.
